nativeRange.habitat: The Orange Spotted Blenny inhabits shallow tropical reef flats and intertidal zones where algal growth is abundant, typically found at depths between 0 and 6 m. Its range spans the Indo-Pacific broadly — from East Africa across to the Society Islands, north to the Ryukyu Islands, and south to approximately 24 degrees S.

decorPreferences: well-established live rock with abundant microalgae growth
caves and crevices for perching and shelter
tightly fitted lid to prevent jumping

diet.notes: Primarily a microalgae grazer that browses film and filamentous algae from live rock and glass. Generally advised to supplement with dried marine algae and spirulina sheets, herbivore-formulated frozen and prepared foods, and occasional meaty offerings; some sources have reported it taking small crustaceans (mysis, krill) and zooplankton. Has been known to slowly starve in tanks scrubbed clean of algae or without an established crop of natural microalgae.

avoidWith: other combtooth blennies of similar appearance
small-polyp stony corals it may nip

summary: A peaceful algae-grazing combtooth blenny from shallow Indo-Pacific reef flats, recognizable by orange-red spots scattered over a pale body. Generally considered reef-safe with caveats and a known jumper.
careNotes: The Orange Spotted Blenny does best in a mature reef tank with abundant live rock that has had time to develop a natural crop of microalgae and filamentous algae. Tanks kept aggressively clean tend to leave this species without enough to graze on, so some algae growth on rock and glass is genuinely beneficial here. Generally peaceful toward other fish, it has been reported to be territorial toward conspecifics and other similarly shaped blennies — keeping only one per tank is generally advised unless a confirmed mated pair is available. It has been known to nip at small-polyp stony corals and the mantles of tridacnid clams when natural algae runs low, so it is commonly considered reef-safe with caution rather than unconditionally reef-safe. Like most blennies, it has been reported to be a capable jumper and a tightly fitted lid is strongly recommended.
breedingNotes: The Orange Spotted Blenny reproduces as an egg-layer with distinct pairing between a male and female. Eggs are demersal and adhesive, anchored to substrate by a filamentous pad, and the larvae are planktonic when they hatch, drifting in shallow coastal waters before settling. The species has rarely if ever been bred successfully in home aquariums, as rearing planktonic larvae through metamorphosis is generally considered difficult and has not been routinely accomplished in captivity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference: family Blenniidae, max size 5 in, min tank 30 US gal, peaceful but keep only one per tank (unless mated pair), reef-safe with caution (may nip SPS polyps and clam mantles), moderate care, herbivore needing microalgae crop, water 72-78 F / pH 8.1-8.4 / SG 1.020-1.025 / dKH 8-12. Also lists trade names Redspotted Blenny and Red-spotted Rockskipper.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy and wild biology cross-check: Family Blenniidae, Order Blenniiformes, max 13.0 cm SL, Indo-Pacific (East Africa to Society Islands, north to Ryukyu Islands, south to 24 S), depth 0-6 m, preferred temp 25.4-29.3 C (mean 28.5 C), oviparous with distinct pairing, demersal adhesive eggs with planktonic larvae, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: fishy.wiki
additional.notes: Independent confirmation of water parameters (22.2-25.6 C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), adult size 13 cm, peaceful with intra-species caveat, herbivorous/omnivorous tendencies, hardy. States min tank 300 L (notably higher than LiveAquaria's 30 gal / ~115 L); flagged as known jumper requiring a secure lid.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Independent confirmation of 300 L minimum tank size, temperature ~26 C, peaceful species kept as a pair (distinct male/female, no sex change noted). Two specialist sources (ReefApp + fishy.wiki) independently citing 300 L resolved the tank-size flag in favour of 300 L over the LiveAquaria 30-gal figure.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Attempted as additional cross-check; search did not surface a current product page for this species (likely out of stock / delisted).

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Attempted as secondary cross-check; returned HTTP 403 and was not usable.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeds as an egg-layer with distinct male-female pairing; demersal adhesive eggs hatch into planktonic larvae that are rarely reared successfully in home aquariums.
